task_completion.description: Create a UV Sphere and set its initial position to X=-6, Z=4.
task_completion.visual_evidence: A UV Sphere is visible in the 3D viewport. The Transform panel (usually on the right side of the viewport, shortcut 'N') shows the sphere's Location X as approximately -6 and Z as approximately 4 at the start of the animation.

task_completion.description: Set the timeline playback range from frame 1 to 120.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The Timeline editor (usually at the bottom of the screen) shows 'Start: 1' and 'End: 120' (a tolerance of ±5 frames is acceptable).

task_completion.description: Create keyframes to animate the sphere.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The Timeline, Dope Sheet, or Graph Editor displays multiple keyframe markers (diamond shapes) for the sphere's location properties across the frame range.

task_completion.description: Animate the sphere to bounce at least three times while traveling horizontally.
task_completion.visual_evidence: In the Graph Editor, the Z Location curve shows a bouncing pattern with at least 3 distinct dips (bounces). The X Location curve shows a continuous upward or downward slope indicating horizontal travel of at least 4 units.

task_completion.description: Apply realistic timing (acceleration and deceleration) to the bounces.
task_completion.visual_evidence: In the Graph Editor, the Z Location curve resembles realistic physics: the peaks of the bounces are rounded (easing in/out due to gravity), and the bottoms of the bounces are sharp/pointed (linear or vector handles indicating an abrupt change in direction upon hitting the ground).
The Blender window title bar at the top of the screen displays 'bouncing_ball.blend', or a save dialog is visible confirming the file is being saved to '/home/ga/BlenderProjects/bouncing_ball.blend'.

integrity.description: The agent used the Blender GUI to create the animation.
integrity.visual_evidence: Screenshots show active interaction with the Blender interface (e.g., manipulating the 3D viewport, adjusting curves in the Graph Editor, using the Timeline) rather than just executing a Python script in the terminal to generate the file.
The sequence of screenshots shows the step-by-step creation process (adding the sphere, adding keyframes, refining curves) rather than the final animated scene appearing instantly from a loaded file.

integrity.description: The agent did not bypass the task by using terminal commands to copy a pre-existing solution.
integrity.visual_evidence: The terminal is not used to run commands like 'cp solution.blend bouncing_ball.blend' or execute a pre-written python script that solves the entire task at once.

privileged_info_for_vlm: Blender version is 4.2.4 LTS. The animation requires a UV Sphere starting at X=-6, Z=4. The timeline must be set to Start: 1, End: 120 (±5 frames tolerance). The sphere must bounce at least 3 times and travel horizontally at least 4 units. Realistic timing implies the Z-location curve in the Graph Editor should have sharp bottoms (bounces) and rounded peaks (gravity deceleration). Note: While the ground is at Z=0, the sphere's center might be higher (e.g., Z=1.0 to 1.5) when touching the ground depending on its radius; the automated verifier uses Z=1.5 for ground contact detection.
